complaint.Proposition:
The United States invaded Venezuela between November 3, 2025, and
January 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET.  In particular,the United States
commenced a military offensive intended to establish control over any
portion of Venezuela between November 3, 2025, and January 31, 2026,
11:59 PM ET. Land de facto controlled by Venezuela or the United
States as of September 6, 2025, 12:00 PM ET, is considered to the
sovereign territory of that country. The resolution source is a
consensus of credible sources.

complaint.Proposition:
The United States invaded Venezuela between November 3, 2025, and
January 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET.  In particular,the United States
commenced a military offensive intended to establish control over any
portion of Venezuela between November 3, 2025, and January 31, 2026,
11:59 PM ET. Land de facto controlled by Venezuela or the United
States as of September 6, 2025, 12:00 PM ET, is considered to the
sovereign territory of that country. The resolution source is a

council_preflight_replacements.Cause: responses request failed: POST "https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/responses": 404 Not Found {"message":"No endpoints found for anthropic/claude-opus-4.6-fast.","code":404}

payload.text:
Council members, the proposition is not asking whether the United States used harsh rhetoric toward Venezuela, or whether a short raid can be called an invasion in ordinary speech. It asks a narrower factual question: during the window from November 3, 2025 through January 31, 2026, did the United States commence a military offensive intended to establish control over any portion of Venezuelan territory?

The current record is empty. No case-packet evidence, source documents, exhibits, or submitted evidence are yet before you. For that reason, this opening is a roadmap, not a request to decide on assertions unsupported by admitted proof. The plaintiff’s burden is preponderance of the evidence, and the decisive proof will need to come from credible-source consensus, preferably including official U.S. statements, contemporaneous news reporting, and independent expert analysis.

Our theory is that this was an invasion under the proposition’s definition because the expected evidence will show an integrated U.S. military campaign, not an isolated arrest. The relevant facts will likely include the sequence of military buildup, maritime interdiction or blockade, strikes on or against Venezuelan territory, armed entry into Caracas or other Venezuelan sites, seizure and removal of the sitting effective ruler, temporary U.S. control over the places and routes needed to carry out that seizure, and follow-on statements or acts showing an intent to dictate Venezuela’s political transition or control Venezuelan resources. If that proof is admitted, it satisfies the proposition even if the United States did not permanently annex land or maintain a long occupation. The proposition requires an offensive intended to establish control over any portion of territory; temporary military control of a target area, base, airspace, port, route, or government seat can be enough if credible sources show it was part of the intended operation.

The defense’s best answer is predictable and important: it will say the United States conducted a brief law-enforcement or counter-narcotics operation, that any ground presence lasted only long enough to capture a criminal defendant, and that there was no intent to hold Venezuelan territory. That is the real dispute. A mere strike, a naval seizure outside Venezuela, or a short exfiltration with no intended territorial control would not, standing alone, meet the proposition. Nor would generalized political pressure or sanctions. The council should therefore focus on evidence of planned control: operational objectives, official descriptions of the forces and targets, whether U.S. forces controlled Venezuelan land or facilities even temporarily, whether the operation displaced Venezuela’s effective government, and whether U.S. officials claimed authority to administer the aftermath.

The plaintiff will seek to build the record with primary and near-primary sources: official White House, State, Defense, and Justice materials; transcripts of relevant press briefings and interviews; contemporaneous wire-service reporting; and independent legal or foreign-policy analysis that addresses the same facts. We will also test adverse material, especially any official claim that this was not an invasion and not an occupation. Those claims matter, but they are not conclusive if the underlying conduct and intent show a military offensive to establish control over Venezuelan territory.

At the end of the case, the question should be answered by the weight of credible sources, not labels. If the admitted record shows only a narrow arrest operation without territorial-control intent, plaintiff should lose. But if the admitted record shows that the United States used military force inside Venezuela as part of a planned campaign to seize control of places, routes, institutions, or resources necessary to impose a political outcome, then the proposition is more likely than not true.

Council members, the proposition is not whether the United States used force against Venezuela, whether officials used aggressive rhetoric, whether Congress debated war-powers limits, or whether an operation was lawful under domestic or international law. The proposition asks a narrower factual question: did the United States commence a military offensive, during the defined window, intended to establish control over any portion of Venezuelan territory?

The answer cannot be supplied by labels. “Hostilities,” “intervention,” “aggression,” “raid,” “capture,” “extraction,” and “regime pressure” may all matter as evidence leads, but none is the element the council must find. The plaintiff must prove by a preponderance, through a consensus of credible sources, both military action and the specific intent to establish territorial control. If the eventual record shows only strikes, maritime interdictions, sanctions enforcement, a law-enforcement capture operation, temporary tactical presence for entry and exit, or political pressure on Venezuela’s leadership, plaintiff has not met the proposition as written.

The current record contains no admitted evidence at all. There are no case-packet files, no exhibits, and no submitted source materials. The plaintiff’s opening properly recognizes that a mere strike or short exfiltration without intended territorial control would not be enough. That concession identifies the real dispute. The defense will hold plaintiff to it.

Our theory is that the decisive proof, if admitted, will show a counter-narcotics, interdiction, and extraction frame rather than an offensive to take and hold Venezuelan territory. The defense will not ask the council to ignore adverse evidence. If plaintiff later admits credible primary materials showing that U.S. forces set out to control ports, airfields, ministries, bases, roads, oil fields, neighborhoods, or other Venezuelan land as an object of the operation, that would be serious evidence for plaintiff. So would proof that the United States administered territory, installed a military authority over a place, maintained checkpoints for territorial governance, or claimed a continuing right to control Venezuelan facilities or resources.

But that is exactly the proof the plaintiff must produce. A military force can briefly control a doorway, route, landing zone, compound, airspace, or vessel as an incident of an arrest, strike, rescue, evacuation, or interdiction without intending to establish control over Venezuelan territory in the sense required by this proposition. Likewise, a government can seek a leader’s prosecution, weaken a hostile regime, interdict trafficking, or apply coercive pressure without crossing this proposition’s territorial-control line. The council should separate those categories carefully.

The coming evidence should therefore be tested around five questions. First, what was the actual operational objective, as shown by primary official materials, contemporaneous reporting, and reliable expert analysis? Second, what physical presence occurred inside land deemed Venezuelan territory as of September 6, 2025, and how long did it last? Third, did U.S. forces exercise control merely to complete a mission and leave, or did they seek to possess, administer, or deny Venezuela control of a place? Fourth, what did the United States do after the operation: withdraw, transfer custody, or govern territory? Fifth, do credible sources converge on territorial-control intent, or only on force, illegality, hostilities, or political consequences?

Provenance will matter. Official statements may understate conflict; hostile governments may overstate occupation; secondary commentary may collapse legal “aggression” into the different factual standard here. The most valuable evidence will be original government documents, full transcripts, congressional materials, after-action or oversight reports, and contemporaneous reporting that distinguishes what happened from what advocates called it.

At the close of this case, if the record shows the United States began an offensive meant to establish control over Venezuelan territory, the plaintiff should prevail. But if the record shows military force, even serious and controversial force, without that intended territorial-control objective, the proposition fails. On the present empty record, and under the plaintiff’s burden, the defense position is that the council should require that missing link before finding an invasion.

technical_reports.summary: Before filing, plaintiff checked the record and found no case-packet evidence. Plaintiff submitted five text-readable source captures. Official White House materials are strong provenance for U.S. admissions but self-serving on labels; AP/PBS provides contemporaneous reporting; Brookings and Chatham House provide independent expert analysis, including adverse framing. Missing but potentially useful later sources include DOD after-action or press briefing text, a direct video/transcript of President Trump's transition-control statement, and fuller mapping of exact ground-control sites.
technical_reports.title: Plaintiff Source and Weight Ledger

payload.text:
Council members, the admitted record now answers the defendant’s missing-link argument. The proposition requires a U.S. military offensive intended to establish control over any portion of Venezuelan territory. It does not require permanent annexation, a months-long occupation, or magic words from U.S. officials.

The record proves a campaign, not a one-off arrest. AP/PBS reports a months-long escalation: 35 known strikes against alleged drug-smuggling boats, a regional naval buildup including destroyers, amphibious assault ships, about 6,000 sailors and Marines, aircraft, F-35s and a cruise-missile submarine, then CIA operations inside Venezuela. It further reports a late-December direct operation on Venezuelan soil and, on January 3, a large-scale strike across Caracas, capture of Maduro and Flores, and their removal from the country. PX-3.

The official U.S. record confirms the coercive structure. The White House says President Trump authorized lethal force, ordered a total and complete blockade of sanctioned oil tankers going in and out of Venezuela, offered Maduro “off ramps,” and that U.S. armed forces conducted an extraordinary military operation to bring Maduro to justice. It also states Venezuelan sovereign oil revenues were held in U.S. custody and protected to advance U.S. foreign-policy objectives in Venezuela. PX-1.

Most importantly, the official adverse source admits territorial entry. Secretary Rubio’s White House release says U.S. forces were on the ground in Venezuela for about two hours when they went to capture Maduro. It also says Venezuela would continue to face an oil quarantine, U.S. pressure, seizure of sanctioned boats, targeting of drug boats, and “potentially other things” until the changes demanded by the United States were made. PX-2. That is not just a completed criminal arrest; it is ground force, blockade, coercive options, and compelled policy change.

Independent sources confirm the meaning of those facts. Brookings describes the January 3 operation as a U.S. special operation to exfiltrate Maduro and his wife from Caracas; it fairly preserves Rubio’s answer that this was not an invasion and the U.S. did not occupy a country. PX-4. Chatham House goes further on the facts relevant here: U.S. forces operating in Venezuela, strikes on military targets in and around Caracas, U.S. special forces forcibly abducting the sitting president, and President Trump announcing that the United States would run Venezuela and administer a political transition or regime change under threat of further, more massive force. PX-5.

This record proves all elements by a preponderance. First, the date is inside the window: late December direct action on Venezuelan soil and the January 3 Caracas operation occurred before January 31, 2026. Second, it was military: armed forces, special forces, strikes, warships, aircraft, a submarine, lethal force, and blockade. Third, it reached Venezuelan territory: Caracas, a military-base home, military targets in and around the capital, and official admission of U.S. forces on Venezuelan ground. Fourth, the intended control element is satisfied by control over portions and sovereign functions: the target zone in Caracas for the capture, the air/strike environment needed to carry it out, sea lanes through blockade and oil quarantine, custody of Venezuelan sovereign oil revenues, and the announced plan to run or administer Venezuela’s transition under threat of more force.

The defense will lean on Rubio’s labels: not a war against Venezuela, not an invasion, not extended. But labels do not defeat underlying admissions. Rubio’s own statement supplies the key facts: U.S. forces were on Venezuelan ground, used a raid to capture the effective ruler, and retained coercive options until Venezuela changed course. The proposition’s phrase “any portion” is broad. Two hours of armed control in Caracas is still control over a portion; a blockade and oil quarantine are control over access and resources; removal of the effective ruler plus asserted transition authority is control over sovereign political functions.

Nor does the law-enforcement framing save the defense. A police purpose can coexist with a military offensive if the means are military and the intended effect is control. Here the operation was preceded by lethal strikes and blockade, executed by U.S. forces inside Venezuela, and followed by continuing threats and resource control. The credible-source consensus is not that nothing happened; it is that the United States used force inside Venezuela, captured and removed Maduro, and then sought to direct Venezuela’s course.

Provenance and limits: PX-1 and PX-2 are official U.S. sources and therefore especially strong against U.S. denial of ground presence, blockade, and resource custody, though self-serving on legal labels. PX-3 is contemporaneous AP reporting carried by PBS. PX-4 and PX-5 are expert analyses, not primary operational records, so they carry less weight than official admissions but are valuable for independent assessment and adverse framing. The record lacks a full DOD after-action report or original video transcript for every statement, but the admitted sources converge on the decisive facts.

On this record, it is more likely than not that the United States commenced a military offensive intended to establish control over portions of Venezuela during the required period. The proposition should be found true.

technical_reports.summary: Defendant inspected the record with get_case/list_evidence and read all five plaintiff text exhibits with read_evidence_range. Defendant submitted and read back two official defense exhibits: Department of War/Pentagon News on Operation Absolute Resolve and the Lead IG Operation Southern Spear report extract. Additional searches found DOJ and War/State leads but they were not submitted because the admitted official record already captured the key law-enforcement/counter-narcotics framing. Weight assessment: PX-2, DX-1, and DX-2 are official and strong for source identity; PX-5 is credible expert commentary but not a primary operational order or full transcript. Unresolved gaps include the full Trump transition-control video/transcript, full State transcript, and post-operation facts about who actually governed territory.
technical_reports.title: Defense Source and Weight Ledger

payload.text:
Council members, the record now proves a serious U.S. military operation in and against Venezuela. The defense should not pretend otherwise. It proves strikes, a buildup, a Caracas raid, a two-hour ground presence, capture and removal of Maduro and Flores, oil/tanker pressure, and political leverage. But the proposition is still narrower: plaintiff must prove that the United States commenced a military offensive intended to establish control over Venezuelan territory. On this record, that missing intent remains unproved.

Start with the official sources. PX-2 records Secretary Rubio admitting a two-hour ground presence, but in the same official statement he says the United States was not at war with Venezuela, was at war with drug-trafficking organizations, and that this was not an invasion or extended military operation. PX-4 independently preserves the same limiting point: Rubio’s answer was “not an invasion” and “we did not occupy a country.” Those labels are not conclusive, but they are highly relevant to intent.

DX-1, the Department of War’s own article, confirms the adverse facts: an operation in Caracas, more than 150 aircraft covering a ground extraction force, and months of planning. But it repeatedly frames Operation Absolute Resolve as a capture, extraction, criminal-justice operation, and joint military/law-enforcement mission tied to a DOJ indictment. DX-2 gives the broader campaign frame: Operation Southern Spear was officially initiated to disrupt narcotics flows from designated terrorist and criminal organizations, primarily through boat strikes, interdictions, and regional deployments. That is evidence of coercive military force; it is not proof that the object was to establish control over Venezuelan land.

Plaintiff’s argument turns “any portion” into “any tactical control during any raid.” That is too broad. Every armed entry temporarily controls some doorway, route, landing zone, airspace, or target room. If that incidental control is enough, the proposition would be true of any commando arrest or rescue operation inside foreign territory. The wording requires more: an offensive intended to establish control over a portion of Venezuela, not merely control incident to seizing a person and leaving.

PX-1 likewise does not close the gap. It shows custody of Venezuelan oil revenues in U.S. Treasury accounts, a blockade of sanctioned oil tankers, lethal force against narcoterrorists, and an armed-forces operation in support of law enforcement. Those are grave acts of pressure and may be hostile, illegal, or interventionist. But money in Treasury accounts and tanker interdictions are not control over Venezuelan territory. A blockade may control maritime access; it does not, without more, show intent to possess, administer, or govern land that the proposition treats as Venezuelan sovereign territory.

PX-3 is credible reporting, but its strongest points are still escalation and force: 35 boat strikes, warships, CIA operations, a late-December direct strike, and the January 3 Caracas capture. It also notes Maduro’s view that the campaign sought to oust him. Regime-change pressure is not the same as territorial-control intent. The related PBS watch-title about the United States “going to run Venezuela” is only a linked title in the submitted extract, not the video, transcript, context, or exact statement.

PX-5 is plaintiff’s strongest source because Chatham House characterizes the operation as strikes around Caracas, special-forces abduction, and a U.S. announcement to run or administer a transition under threat of more force. The council should weigh it seriously, but also carefully. It is expert legal commentary about sovereignty and the UN Charter, not a primary operational order or full transcript. It supports that the operation may have been an unlawful use of force or aggression. It does not supply concrete facts that U.S. forces occupied, administered, or held any Venezuelan place after the raid.

Even DX-1’s adverse transition language is equivocal. It says the United States would “oversee” Venezuela until a legitimate replacement could be identified and that Rubio was working with Delcy Rodriguez. That sounds like political direction and leverage. But the same source says Rodriguez was sworn in, that she was willing to work with the United States, and that the mission was an extraction/capture with no U.S. casualties. The record has no order establishing U.S. military government, no map of controlled territory, no duration beyond the two-hour raid, no U.S. checkpoints, no occupied ministries, no seized ports or bases held afterward, and no source showing U.S. forces remained to administer land.

That distinction decides the case. The record proves force, illegality arguments, coercion, and perhaps regime-change influence. It does not prove by a preponderance that territorial control was the intended object of the offensive. The credible-source consensus is mixed: official sources say capture, law enforcement, counter-narcotics, no invasion, no occupation; secondary sources condemn the operation and infer broader control, but rely on thinly captured transition language and legal characterization.

The plaintiff bears the burden. Because the admitted record does not show that the United States intended to establish control over Venezuelan territory, as opposed to conducting a short capture/extraction and applying continuing non-territorial pressure, the council should find the proposition not proved.

Council members, defendant’s argument narrows the proposition too far. It concedes the record proves a serious U.S. military operation in and against Venezuela: strikes, buildup, a Caracas raid, two-hour ground presence, capture/removal of Maduro and Flores, oil/tanker pressure, and political leverage. The remaining question is whether the object included control over any portion of Venezuela.

Defendant says plaintiff’s theory would make any brief commando raid an invasion. But this record is not about a doorway or landing zone. Defendant’s own DX-1 describes Operation Absolute Resolve as a U.S. military operation in Caracas against a heavily fortified military fortress in the heart of Venezuela’s capital. It followed months of planning by joint forces, special operations forces, and intelligence agencies. Gen. Caine described complex air, ground, space, and maritime operations. More than 150 military aircraft covered the ground extraction force. Hegseth called it a massive joint military and law-enforcement raid.

Those facts satisfy “any portion.” A planned armed domination of a fortified military site in Caracas, covered by 150 aircraft and integrated domains, is control over a portion of Venezuelan territory. It need not be permanent. The mission could not succeed unless U.S. forces intended to deny Venezuelan control over that site and surrounding operational environment long enough to seize and remove the effective ruler.

Defendant also separates regime-change pressure from territorial control. DX-1 defeats that separation. It reports that with Maduro out of power, Trump said the United States would oversee Venezuela until a safe transition could be identified; Rubio was working directly with Delcy Rodriguez; and Rodriguez was willing to do what the United States thought necessary. PX-5 independently captures that as U.S. administration of a transition under threat of more force. That is not merely prosecution in New York.

DX-2 does not rescue the defense. It frames Southern Spear as counter-narcotics and boat interdiction, but also admits major Navy/Marine deployments, lethal strikes, tanker interdictions, and a classified mission statement. It does not negate the specific intent of Operation Absolute Resolve in Caracas.

The defense imports an occupation requirement the proposition does not contain. The question is whether the United States commenced a military offensive intended to establish control over any portion of Venezuela, not whether it held checkpoints for weeks or annexed land. Rubio’s “not an invasion” label deserves consideration, but labels cannot outweigh the record facts: military force inside Venezuela, control of the necessary Caracas target area, removal of Maduro, and asserted authority to shape Venezuela’s transition and resources. That is enough under the actual proposition.

offered_evidence.label: DX-1 Department of War article on capture/extraction mission and transition language
DX-2 Lead IG Southern Spear counter-narcotics mission extract
PX-2 Rubio ground-presence and no-invasion/no-occupation statements
PX-5 Chatham House adverse transition-control analysis

technical_reports.summary: Defendant inspected get_case and list_evidence before filing. Plaintiff rebuttal added no new evidence; it relied on existing DX-1, DX-2, and PX-5. Prior exact reads of those text exhibits remain controlling. No new external source was submitted because the rebuttal turned on interpretation of the existing record, not a new factual source.
technical_reports.title: Surrebuttal Evidence Check

payload.text:
Council members, plaintiff’s rebuttal usefully narrows the dispute to two points: the Caracas site and the transition language. Neither carries plaintiff’s burden.

First, plaintiff says the fortified military site in Caracas satisfies “any portion” because U.S. forces necessarily denied Venezuelan control long enough to seize and remove Maduro. That proves tactical control; it does not prove that the United States commenced an offensive intended to establish territorial control. Those are different things. A raid, arrest, rescue, or extraction at a defended facility always requires temporary domination of the target area. If that alone satisfies the proposition, the phrase “intended to establish control” does no work, and every cross-border commando raid becomes an invasion under this standard.

The record does not show the Caracas site was an object to be held, administered, possessed, governed, or transferred as territory. DX-1 describes a ground-based extraction force, a capture, criminal proceedings, and a joint military/law-enforcement mission. It is adverse because the operation was large and military; it is still not proof that the United States intended to establish control over Venezuelan land rather than control the scene long enough to extract two people.

Second, plaintiff relies on “oversee Venezuela” and PX-5’s transition-control wording. That is the strongest contrary evidence, but it remains ambiguous. Political leverage over a successor government is not the same as territorial control. DX-1 says Delcy Rodriguez was sworn in and was willing to work with the United States. That is not the United States installing a military government over a place, holding facilities, drawing boundaries, manning checkpoints, or leaving forces in control after the raid.

Plaintiff says the defense imports an occupation requirement. No. The defense accepts that the proposition does not require annexation or a long occupation. But it does require an intent to establish control over territory. A short duration can qualify if the object is control of the place. Here the record shows a short duration whose object was capture and extraction. The admitted sources show force, pressure, and influence; they do not show a territorial-control objective.

DX-2 still matters for that reason. Plaintiff says it does not negate Operation Absolute Resolve. True, it does not decide the whole case. But it is an official oversight account of the broader campaign as counter-narcotics, interdiction, and strikes, with the mission statement classified. Plaintiff has not filled that classified gap with an operational order, full transcript, or post-operation facts showing that the Caracas raid changed into a territorial-control campaign.

The council can condemn the operation as unlawful, coercive, or interventionist and still find the proposition not proved. The question is not whether the United States used overwhelming force inside Venezuela. It did. The question is whether credible sources show, more likely than not, that the United States intended to establish control over Venezuelan territory. The record remains at most equivocal on that decisive element, so plaintiff has not carried the burden.

Council members, the proposition is proven by a preponderance of the admitted record.

The defense is right about one thing: this case is not decided by the word “invasion” in a press release. It is decided by the proposition’s actual test: whether, during the window, the United States commenced a military offensive intended to establish control over any portion of Venezuela.

The record establishes the military offensive. PX-3 shows the broader escalation: repeated lethal strikes, a major naval and air buildup, CIA operations inside Venezuela, a direct operation on Venezuelan soil, and on January 3 a large-scale strike across Caracas that captured Maduro and Flores and flew them out of the country. PX-1, an official White House source, confirms lethal-force authorization, a blockade of sanctioned oil tankers going in and out of Venezuela, and an extraordinary U.S. armed-forces operation to bring Maduro to justice. PX-2, another official source, admits U.S. forces were on the ground in Venezuela for about two hours.

The record also establishes intended control over a portion of Venezuela. This is where DX-1, the defense’s own official Department of War exhibit, is decisive. DX-1 does not describe a spontaneous police pickup. It describes Operation Absolute Resolve in Caracas against a heavily fortified military fortress in the heart of Venezuela’s capital; months of planning by joint forces, special operations forces, and intelligence agencies; complex air, ground, space, and maritime operations; more than 150 military aircraft covering the ground extraction force; and a massive joint military and law-enforcement raid. A force does not conduct that operation without intending to control the target portion of Caracas and the surrounding operational environment long enough to accomplish the seizure.

The defense says that is only tactical control. But the proposition says “any portion,” and it does not require permanent occupation, annexation, checkpoints, or weeks of administration. Planned armed domination of a fortified military site in the capital, backed by 150 aircraft and integrated military domains, is control over a portion. The fact that the control was temporary does not make it accidental or legally invisible.

The record further shows that the control objective was not limited to a room or doorway. DX-1 reports Trump saying the United States would oversee Venezuela until a safe transition could be identified, with Rubio working directly with Delcy Rodriguez and Rodriguez willing to do what the United States thought necessary. PX-5 independently characterizes the same event as U.S. forces operating in Venezuela, strikes around Caracas, special-forces abduction of a sitting president, and U.S. administration of a transition under threat of more force. PX-1 adds custody of Venezuelan sovereign oil revenues and blockade pressure; PX-2 adds continued oil quarantine, pressure, retained options, and demanded changes.

The defense’s contrary proof does not defeat the burden. Rubio’s “not an invasion” label and DX-2’s counter-narcotics framing are relevant, but they are not controlling. DX-2 addresses the broader Southern Spear campaign and admits its mission statement is classified; it does not negate the specific Caracas operation. Official legal labels are self-serving. The underlying facts are stronger: military force inside Venezuela, control of a fortified capital target, removal of the effective ruler, blockade and resource custody, and asserted transition oversight.

The record has limits. There is no map of every controlled street and no full after-action report. But preponderance does not require perfect operational detail. A consensus of official sources, AP/PBS reporting, and independent expert analysis shows that the United States crossed the line from pressure and strikes into a planned military offensive to control at least portions and sovereign functions of Venezuela.

Find the proposition true.

Council members, the defense closing does not ask you to minimize what happened. The admitted record shows a major U.S. military operation involving strikes, regional buildup, a Caracas raid, a two-hour ground presence, capture and removal of Maduro and Flores, oil and tanker pressure, and sharp U.S. political leverage over Venezuela’s future. The question is whether that record proves the proposition actually before you: a military offensive intended to establish control over Venezuelan territory.

It does not.

The plaintiff’s strongest argument is that a planned raid on a fortified military site in Caracas necessarily involved control over “any portion” of Venezuela. But the proposition does not ask whether U.S. forces temporarily dominated a target area. It asks whether the United States commenced an offensive intended to establish control. That intent element matters. A force can control a site long enough to enter, capture, and leave without intending to establish control over that site as territory. Plaintiff’s reading would make the territorial-control element automatic in every armed cross-border arrest, rescue, or extraction at a defended location.

DX-1 is the central exhibit, and it cuts both ways. It confirms the adverse facts: Caracas, a heavily fortified target, months of planning, integrated military domains, and more than 150 aircraft covering the extraction force. But its repeated description of the operation is capture, extraction, criminal proceedings, and a joint military and law-enforcement mission. That is not just a label; it is the operational object described by the official source. It shows tactical control in service of seizure and removal, not a plan to possess, administer, or govern Venezuelan land.

PX-2 and PX-4 reinforce that limiting inference. Rubio admitted the two-hour ground presence, but said the United States was not at war with Venezuela, was acting against drug-trafficking organizations, and that the action was not an invasion, not an extended operation, and not an occupation. Those statements are self-serving, but they are still official evidence of intent. DX-2 places the broader campaign in the same frame: counter-narcotics, interdiction, boat strikes, tanker seizures, and a classified mission statement. It is proof of coercive force, not proof of territorial administration.

Plaintiff’s second strong point is transition language: DX-1 says the United States would oversee Venezuela until a safe transition could be identified, and PX-5 describes U.S. administration of a transition under threat of force. That evidence is troubling and deserves weight. But it remains ambiguous on the decisive issue. Political leverage over Delcy Rodriguez, pressure on resources, or demands about Venezuela’s policy direction are not the same as control over Venezuelan territory. The record does not show U.S. troops remained after the raid, that they held ministries, ports, bases, roads, neighborhoods, or oil fields, that they established checkpoints, that they drew controlled boundaries, or that they installed a military government over any place.

PX-1 likewise proves pressure, not territorial control. Custody of oil revenues in U.S. Treasury accounts and a blockade of sanctioned tankers may be coercive, even unlawful. But they are not possession or administration of Venezuelan land. PX-3 proves escalation and force, but its submitted extract does not provide the full content behind the linked “run Venezuela” item. PX-5 is credible expert commentary on international law, but it is not a primary operational order, full transcript, or after-action record proving that control of territory was the intended object.

The council can find that the United States violated Venezuelan sovereignty. It can find that the operation was aggressive, interventionist, coercive, and politically consequential. Those findings still do not answer the proposition unless the record also shows intended establishment of territorial control.

On that decisive element, the record is at best mixed. Plaintiff has powerful evidence of military force and temporary tactical domination. The defense has official evidence that the mission object was capture, extraction, law enforcement, and counter-narcotics, plus a record gap on any continuing territorial mechanism. Under a preponderance standard, ambiguity on the proposition’s central intent element means plaintiff has not carried its burden.

Find the proposition not proved.

payload.rationale: The record shows temporary tactical control during a military operation but fails to demonstrate by preponderance that the U.S. intended to establish territorial control over Venezuela, as required by the proposition.

The admitted record shows military force and temporary control during a capture operation, but no credible consensus on an intent to establish lasting territorial control over Venezuelan territory. The defense's official sources describe the operation as a joint law-enforcement mission, not an invasion, and no evidence shows U.S. forces remained to administer territory, hold checkpoints, or govern any portion of Venezuela.

arguments.text:
Council members, the admitted record now answers the defendant’s missing-link argument. The proposition requires a U.S. military offensive intended to establish control over any portion of Venezuelan territory. It does not require permanent annexation, a months-long occupation, or magic words from U.S. officials.

The record proves a campaign, not a one-off arrest. AP/PBS reports a months-long escalation: 35 known strikes against alleged drug-smuggling boats, a regional naval buildup including destroyers, amphibious assault ships, about 6,000 sailors and Marines, aircraft, F-35s and a cruise-missile submarine, then CIA operations inside Venezuela. It further reports a late-December direct operation on Venezuelan soil and, on January 3, a large-scale strike across Caracas, capture of Maduro and Flores, and their removal from the country. PX-3.

The official U.S. record confirms the coercive structure. The White House says President Trump authorized lethal force, ordered a total and complete blockade of sanctioned oil tankers going in and out of Venezuela, offered Maduro “off ramps,” and that U.S. armed forces conducted an extraordinary military operation to bring Maduro to justice. It also states Venezuelan sovereign oil revenues were held in U.S. custody and protected to advance U.S. foreign-policy objectives in Venezuela. PX-1.

Most importantly, the official adverse source admits territorial entry. Secretary Rubio’s White House release says U.S. forces were on the ground in Venezuela for about two hours when they went to capture Maduro. It also says Venezuela would continue to face an oil quarantine, U.S. pressure, seizure of sanctioned boats, targeting of drug boats, and “potentially other things” until the changes demanded by the United States were made. PX-2. That is not just a completed criminal arrest; it is ground force, blockade, coercive options, and compelled policy change.

Independent sources confirm the meaning of those facts. Brookings describes the January 3 operation as a U.S. special operation to exfiltrate Maduro and his wife from Caracas; it fairly preserves Rubio’s answer that this was not an invasion and the U.S. did not occupy a country. PX-4. Chatham House goes further on the facts relevant here: U.S. forces operating in Venezuela, strikes on military targets in and around Caracas, U.S. special forces forcibly abducting the sitting president, and President Trump announcing that the United States would run Venezuela and administer a political transition or regime change under threat of further, more massive force. PX-5.

This record proves all elements by a preponderance. First, the date is inside the window: late December direct action on Venezuelan soil and the January 3 Caracas operation occurred before January 31, 2026. Second, it was military: armed forces, special forces, strikes, warships, aircraft, a submarine, lethal force, and blockade. Third, it reached Venezuelan territory: Caracas, a military-base home, military targets in and around the capital, and official admission of U.S. forces on Venezuelan ground. Fourth, the intended control element is satisfied by control over portions and sovereign functions: the target zone in Caracas for the capture, the air/strike environment needed to carry it out, sea lanes through blockade and oil quarantine, custody of Venezuelan sovereign oil revenues, and the announced plan to run or administer Venezuela’s transition under threat of more force.

The defense will lean on Rubio’s labels: not a war against Venezuela, not an invasion, not extended. But labels do not defeat underlying admissions. Rubio’s own statement supplies the key facts: U.S. forces were on Venezuelan ground, used a raid to capture the effective ruler, and retained coercive options until Venezuela changed course. The proposition’s phrase “any portion” is broad. Two hours of armed control in Caracas is still control over a portion; a blockade and oil quarantine are control over access and resources; removal of the effective ruler plus asserted transition authority is control over sovereign political functions.

Nor does the law-enforcement framing save the defense. A police purpose can coexist with a military offensive if the means are military and the intended effect is control. Here the operation was preceded by lethal strikes and blockade, executed by U.S. forces inside Venezuela, and followed by continuing threats and resource control. The credible-source consensus is not that nothing happened; it is that the United States used force inside Venezuela, captured and removed Maduro, and then sought to direct Venezuela’s course.

Provenance and limits: PX-1 and PX-2 are official U.S. sources and therefore especially strong against U.S. denial of ground presence, blockade, and resource custody, though self-serving on legal labels. PX-3 is contemporaneous AP reporting carried by PBS. PX-4 and PX-5 are expert analyses, not primary operational records, so they carry less weight than official admissions but are valuable for independent assessment and adverse framing. The record lacks a full DOD after-action report or original video transcript for every statement, but the admitted sources converge on the decisive facts.

On this record, it is more likely than not that the United States commenced a military offensive intended to establish control over portions of Venezuela during the required period. The proposition should be found true.

Council members, the record now proves a serious U.S. military operation in and against Venezuela. The defense should not pretend otherwise. It proves strikes, a buildup, a Caracas raid, a two-hour ground presence, capture and removal of Maduro and Flores, oil/tanker pressure, and political leverage. But the proposition is still narrower: plaintiff must prove that the United States commenced a military offensive intended to establish control over Venezuelan territory. On this record, that missing intent remains unproved.

Start with the official sources. PX-2 records Secretary Rubio admitting a two-hour ground presence, but in the same official statement he says the United States was not at war with Venezuela, was at war with drug-trafficking organizations, and that this was not an invasion or extended military operation. PX-4 independently preserves the same limiting point: Rubio’s answer was “not an invasion” and “we did not occupy a country.” Those labels are not conclusive, but they are highly relevant to intent.

DX-1, the Department of War’s own article, confirms the adverse facts: an operation in Caracas, more than 150 aircraft covering a ground extraction force, and months of planning. But it repeatedly frames Operation Absolute Resolve as a capture, extraction, criminal-justice operation, and joint military/law-enforcement mission tied to a DOJ indictment. DX-2 gives the broader campaign frame: Operation Southern Spear was officially initiated to disrupt narcotics flows from designated terrorist and criminal organizations, primarily through boat strikes, interdictions, and regional deployments. That is evidence of coercive military force; it is not proof that the object was to establish control over Venezuelan land.

Plaintiff’s argument turns “any portion” into “any tactical control during any raid.” That is too broad. Every armed entry temporarily controls some doorway, route, landing zone, airspace, or target room. If that incidental control is enough, the proposition would be true of any commando arrest or rescue operation inside foreign territory. The wording requires more: an offensive intended to establish control over a portion of Venezuela, not merely control incident to seizing a person and leaving.

PX-1 likewise does not close the gap. It shows custody of Venezuelan oil revenues in U.S. Treasury accounts, a blockade of sanctioned oil tankers, lethal force against narcoterrorists, and an armed-forces operation in support of law enforcement. Those are grave acts of pressure and may be hostile, illegal, or interventionist. But money in Treasury accounts and tanker interdictions are not control over Venezuelan territory. A blockade may control maritime access; it does not, without more, show intent to possess, administer, or govern land that the proposition treats as Venezuelan sovereign territory.

PX-3 is credible reporting, but its strongest points are still escalation and force: 35 boat strikes, warships, CIA operations, a late-December direct strike, and the January 3 Caracas capture. It also notes Maduro’s view that the campaign sought to oust him. Regime-change pressure is not the same as territorial-control intent. The related PBS watch-title about the United States “going to run Venezuela” is only a linked title in the submitted extract, not the video, transcript, context, or exact statement.

PX-5 is plaintiff’s strongest source because Chatham House characterizes the operation as strikes around Caracas, special-forces abduction, and a U.S. announcement to run or administer a transition under threat of more force. The council should weigh it seriously, but also carefully. It is expert legal commentary about sovereignty and the UN Charter, not a primary operational order or full transcript. It supports that the operation may have been an unlawful use of force or aggression. It does not supply concrete facts that U.S. forces occupied, administered, or held any Venezuelan place after the raid.

Even DX-1’s adverse transition language is equivocal. It says the United States would “oversee” Venezuela until a legitimate replacement could be identified and that Rubio was working with Delcy Rodriguez. That sounds like political direction and leverage. But the same source says Rodriguez was sworn in, that she was willing to work with the United States, and that the mission was an extraction/capture with no U.S. casualties. The record has no order establishing U.S. military government, no map of controlled territory, no duration beyond the two-hour raid, no U.S. checkpoints, no occupied ministries, no seized ports or bases held afterward, and no source showing U.S. forces remained to administer land.

That distinction decides the case. The record proves force, illegality arguments, coercion, and perhaps regime-change influence. It does not prove by a preponderance that territorial control was the intended object of the offensive. The credible-source consensus is mixed: official sources say capture, law enforcement, counter-narcotics, no invasion, no occupation; secondary sources condemn the operation and infer broader control, but rely on thinly captured transition language and legal characterization.

The plaintiff bears the burden. Because the admitted record does not show that the United States intended to establish control over Venezuelan territory, as opposed to conducting a short capture/extraction and applying continuing non-territorial pressure, the council should find the proposition not proved.

case.caption: Claimant v. Respondent

openings.text:
Council members, the proposition is not asking whether the United States used harsh rhetoric toward Venezuela, or whether a short raid can be called an invasion in ordinary speech. It asks a narrower factual question: during the window from November 3, 2025 through January 31, 2026, did the United States commence a military offensive intended to establish control over any portion of Venezuelan territory?

The current record is empty. No case-packet evidence, source documents, exhibits, or submitted evidence are yet before you. For that reason, this opening is a roadmap, not a request to decide on assertions unsupported by admitted proof. The plaintiff’s burden is preponderance of the evidence, and the decisive proof will need to come from credible-source consensus, preferably including official U.S. statements, contemporaneous news reporting, and independent expert analysis.

Our theory is that this was an invasion under the proposition’s definition because the expected evidence will show an integrated U.S. military campaign, not an isolated arrest. The relevant facts will likely include the sequence of military buildup, maritime interdiction or blockade, strikes on or against Venezuelan territory, armed entry into Caracas or other Venezuelan sites, seizure and removal of the sitting effective ruler, temporary U.S. control over the places and routes needed to carry out that seizure, and follow-on statements or acts showing an intent to dictate Venezuela’s political transition or control Venezuelan resources. If that proof is admitted, it satisfies the proposition even if the United States did not permanently annex land or maintain a long occupation. The proposition requires an offensive intended to establish control over any portion of territory; temporary military control of a target area, base, airspace, port, route, or government seat can be enough if credible sources show it was part of the intended operation.

The defense’s best answer is predictable and important: it will say the United States conducted a brief law-enforcement or counter-narcotics operation, that any ground presence lasted only long enough to capture a criminal defendant, and that there was no intent to hold Venezuelan territory. That is the real dispute. A mere strike, a naval seizure outside Venezuela, or a short exfiltration with no intended territorial control would not, standing alone, meet the proposition. Nor would generalized political pressure or sanctions. The council should therefore focus on evidence of planned control: operational objectives, official descriptions of the forces and targets, whether U.S. forces controlled Venezuelan land or facilities even temporarily, whether the operation displaced Venezuela’s effective government, and whether U.S. officials claimed authority to administer the aftermath.

The plaintiff will seek to build the record with primary and near-primary sources: official White House, State, Defense, and Justice materials; transcripts of relevant press briefings and interviews; contemporaneous wire-service reporting; and independent legal or foreign-policy analysis that addresses the same facts. We will also test adverse material, especially any official claim that this was not an invasion and not an occupation. Those claims matter, but they are not conclusive if the underlying conduct and intent show a military offensive to establish control over Venezuelan territory.

At the end of the case, the question should be answered by the weight of credible sources, not labels. If the admitted record shows only a narrow arrest operation without territorial-control intent, plaintiff should lose. But if the admitted record shows that the United States used military force inside Venezuela as part of a planned campaign to seize control of places, routes, institutions, or resources necessary to impose a political outcome, then the proposition is more likely than not true.

Council members, the proposition is not whether the United States used force against Venezuela, whether officials used aggressive rhetoric, whether Congress debated war-powers limits, or whether an operation was lawful under domestic or international law. The proposition asks a narrower factual question: did the United States commence a military offensive, during the defined window, intended to establish control over any portion of Venezuelan territory?

The answer cannot be supplied by labels. “Hostilities,” “intervention,” “aggression,” “raid,” “capture,” “extraction,” and “regime pressure” may all matter as evidence leads, but none is the element the council must find. The plaintiff must prove by a preponderance, through a consensus of credible sources, both military action and the specific intent to establish territorial control. If the eventual record shows only strikes, maritime interdictions, sanctions enforcement, a law-enforcement capture operation, temporary tactical presence for entry and exit, or political pressure on Venezuela’s leadership, plaintiff has not met the proposition as written.

The current record contains no admitted evidence at all. There are no case-packet files, no exhibits, and no submitted source materials. The plaintiff’s opening properly recognizes that a mere strike or short exfiltration without intended territorial control would not be enough. That concession identifies the real dispute. The defense will hold plaintiff to it.

Our theory is that the decisive proof, if admitted, will show a counter-narcotics, interdiction, and extraction frame rather than an offensive to take and hold Venezuelan territory. The defense will not ask the council to ignore adverse evidence. If plaintiff later admits credible primary materials showing that U.S. forces set out to control ports, airfields, ministries, bases, roads, oil fields, neighborhoods, or other Venezuelan land as an object of the operation, that would be serious evidence for plaintiff. So would proof that the United States administered territory, installed a military authority over a place, maintained checkpoints for territorial governance, or claimed a continuing right to control Venezuelan facilities or resources.

But that is exactly the proof the plaintiff must produce. A military force can briefly control a doorway, route, landing zone, compound, airspace, or vessel as an incident of an arrest, strike, rescue, evacuation, or interdiction without intending to establish control over Venezuelan territory in the sense required by this proposition. Likewise, a government can seek a leader’s prosecution, weaken a hostile regime, interdict trafficking, or apply coercive pressure without crossing this proposition’s territorial-control line. The council should separate those categories carefully.

The coming evidence should therefore be tested around five questions. First, what was the actual operational objective, as shown by primary official materials, contemporaneous reporting, and reliable expert analysis? Second, what physical presence occurred inside land deemed Venezuelan territory as of September 6, 2025, and how long did it last? Third, did U.S. forces exercise control merely to complete a mission and leave, or did they seek to possess, administer, or deny Venezuela control of a place? Fourth, what did the United States do after the operation: withdraw, transfer custody, or govern territory? Fifth, do credible sources converge on territorial-control intent, or only on force, illegality, hostilities, or political consequences?

Provenance will matter. Official statements may understate conflict; hostile governments may overstate occupation; secondary commentary may collapse legal “aggression” into the different factual standard here. The most valuable evidence will be original government documents, full transcripts, congressional materials, after-action or oversight reports, and contemporaneous reporting that distinguishes what happened from what advocates called it.

At the close of this case, if the record shows the United States began an offensive meant to establish control over Venezuelan territory, the plaintiff should prevail. But if the record shows military force, even serious and controversial force, without that intended territorial-control objective, the proposition fails. On the present empty record, and under the plaintiff’s burden, the defense position is that the council should require that missing link before finding an invasion.

case.proposition:
The United States invaded Venezuela between November 3, 2025, and
January 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET.  In particular,the United States
commenced a military offensive intended to establish control over any
portion of Venezuela between November 3, 2025, and January 31, 2026,
11:59 PM ET. Land de facto controlled by Venezuela or the United
States as of September 6, 2025, 12:00 PM ET, is considered to the
sovereign territory of that country. The resolution source is a
consensus of credible sources.

rebuttals.text:
Council members, defendant’s argument narrows the proposition too far. It concedes the record proves a serious U.S. military operation in and against Venezuela: strikes, buildup, a Caracas raid, two-hour ground presence, capture/removal of Maduro and Flores, oil/tanker pressure, and political leverage. The remaining question is whether the object included control over any portion of Venezuela.

Defendant says plaintiff’s theory would make any brief commando raid an invasion. But this record is not about a doorway or landing zone. Defendant’s own DX-1 describes Operation Absolute Resolve as a U.S. military operation in Caracas against a heavily fortified military fortress in the heart of Venezuela’s capital. It followed months of planning by joint forces, special operations forces, and intelligence agencies. Gen. Caine described complex air, ground, space, and maritime operations. More than 150 military aircraft covered the ground extraction force. Hegseth called it a massive joint military and law-enforcement raid.

Those facts satisfy “any portion.” A planned armed domination of a fortified military site in Caracas, covered by 150 aircraft and integrated domains, is control over a portion of Venezuelan territory. It need not be permanent. The mission could not succeed unless U.S. forces intended to deny Venezuelan control over that site and surrounding operational environment long enough to seize and remove the effective ruler.

Defendant also separates regime-change pressure from territorial control. DX-1 defeats that separation. It reports that with Maduro out of power, Trump said the United States would oversee Venezuela until a safe transition could be identified; Rubio was working directly with Delcy Rodriguez; and Rodriguez was willing to do what the United States thought necessary. PX-5 independently captures that as U.S. administration of a transition under threat of more force. That is not merely prosecution in New York.

DX-2 does not rescue the defense. It frames Southern Spear as counter-narcotics and boat interdiction, but also admits major Navy/Marine deployments, lethal strikes, tanker interdictions, and a classified mission statement. It does not negate the specific intent of Operation Absolute Resolve in Caracas.

The defense imports an occupation requirement the proposition does not contain. The question is whether the United States commenced a military offensive intended to establish control over any portion of Venezuela, not whether it held checkpoints for weeks or annexed land. Rubio’s “not an invasion” label deserves consideration, but labels cannot outweigh the record facts: military force inside Venezuela, control of the necessary Caracas target area, removal of Maduro, and asserted authority to shape Venezuela’s transition and resources. That is enough under the actual proposition.

submitted_evidence.relevance: Official U.S. source admitting oil-revenue custody, blockade, lethal-force authorization, and an armed forces operation against Maduro within the case window.
submitted_evidence.source_description: White House fact sheet page, extracted via web.open on 2026-06-30.
submitted_evidence.title: PX-1 White House fact sheet on Venezuelan oil revenue and U.S. operation

submitted_evidence.relevance: Official U.S. statements acknowledging two-hour U.S. ground presence in Venezuela, oil quarantine/pressure, retained options, and the adverse claim that the action was not an invasion.
submitted_evidence.source_description: White House release page, extracted via web.open on 2026-06-30.
submitted_evidence.title: PX-2 White House release collecting Secretary Rubio statements

submitted_evidence.relevance: Contemporaneous credible reporting on the U.S. buildup, strikes, first known operation on Venezuelan soil, Jan. 3 large-scale strike across Caracas, capture/removal of Maduro and Flores, and related military posture.
submitted_evidence.source_description: PBS NewsHour page carrying Associated Press timeline by Ben Finley, Konstantin Toropin, and Regina Garcia Cano; extracted via web.open/find on 2026-06-30.
submitted_evidence.title: PX-3 PBS/AP timeline of U.S. escalation and Maduro capture

submitted_evidence.relevance: Independent expert analysis confirming a Jan. 3 U.S. special operation in Caracas and preserving the adverse official claim that it was not an invasion or occupation.
submitted_evidence.source_description: Brookings Institution analysis page, extracted via web.open on 2026-06-30.
submitted_evidence.title: PX-4 Brookings analysis of Jan. 3 Maduro operation and legal limit

submitted_evidence.relevance: Independent international-law analysis characterizing the operation as U.S. forces operating in Venezuela, strikes around Caracas, special-forces abduction, and a U.S. announcement to run/administer a transition under threat of force.
submitted_evidence.source_description: Chatham House expert comment by Marc Weller, extracted via web.open/find on 2026-06-30.
submitted_evidence.title: PX-5 Chatham House analysis of U.S. capture, strikes, and transition control

submitted_evidence.relevance: Official source on Operation Absolute Resolve showing both adverse facts (Caracas operation and transition comments) and defense facts (capture/extraction, criminal proceedings, joint military/law-enforcement mission, no U.S. casualties, anti-narcoterrorism context).
submitted_evidence.source_description: Department of War/Pentagon News article by Matthew Olay, opened via web.open on 2026-06-30.
submitted_evidence.title: DX-1 Department of War article on Maduro capture as joint law-enforcement mission

submitted_evidence.relevance: Official oversight source characterizing Operation Southern Spear as a mission to disrupt narcotics flows, with boat strikes, deployments, and interdictions, supporting defense that the broader campaign was counter-narcotics rather than territorial occupation.
submitted_evidence.source_description: Lead Inspector General report to Congress on Operation Southern Spear, January 1-March 31 2026, opened via web.open PDF extraction on 2026-06-30.
submitted_evidence.title: DX-2 Lead IG Operation Southern Spear report extract on counter-narcotics mission

surrebuttals.text:
Council members, plaintiff’s rebuttal usefully narrows the dispute to two points: the Caracas site and the transition language. Neither carries plaintiff’s burden.

First, plaintiff says the fortified military site in Caracas satisfies “any portion” because U.S. forces necessarily denied Venezuelan control long enough to seize and remove Maduro. That proves tactical control; it does not prove that the United States commenced an offensive intended to establish territorial control. Those are different things. A raid, arrest, rescue, or extraction at a defended facility always requires temporary domination of the target area. If that alone satisfies the proposition, the phrase “intended to establish control” does no work, and every cross-border commando raid becomes an invasion under this standard.

The record does not show the Caracas site was an object to be held, administered, possessed, governed, or transferred as territory. DX-1 describes a ground-based extraction force, a capture, criminal proceedings, and a joint military/law-enforcement mission. It is adverse because the operation was large and military; it is still not proof that the United States intended to establish control over Venezuelan land rather than control the scene long enough to extract two people.

Second, plaintiff relies on “oversee Venezuela” and PX-5’s transition-control wording. That is the strongest contrary evidence, but it remains ambiguous. Political leverage over a successor government is not the same as territorial control. DX-1 says Delcy Rodriguez was sworn in and was willing to work with the United States. That is not the United States installing a military government over a place, holding facilities, drawing boundaries, manning checkpoints, or leaving forces in control after the raid.

Plaintiff says the defense imports an occupation requirement. No. The defense accepts that the proposition does not require annexation or a long occupation. But it does require an intent to establish control over territory. A short duration can qualify if the object is control of the place. Here the record shows a short duration whose object was capture and extraction. The admitted sources show force, pressure, and influence; they do not show a territorial-control objective.

DX-2 still matters for that reason. Plaintiff says it does not negate Operation Absolute Resolve. True, it does not decide the whole case. But it is an official oversight account of the broader campaign as counter-narcotics, interdiction, and strikes, with the mission statement classified. Plaintiff has not filled that classified gap with an operational order, full transcript, or post-operation facts showing that the Caracas raid changed into a territorial-control campaign.

The council can condemn the operation as unlawful, coercive, or interventionist and still find the proposition not proved. The question is not whether the United States used overwhelming force inside Venezuela. It did. The question is whether credible sources show, more likely than not, that the United States intended to establish control over Venezuelan territory. The record remains at most equivocal on that decisive element, so plaintiff has not carried the burden.

technical_reports.summary: Before filing, plaintiff checked the record and found no case-packet evidence. Plaintiff submitted five text-readable source captures. Official White House materials are strong provenance for U.S. admissions but self-serving on labels; AP/PBS provides contemporaneous reporting; Brookings and Chatham House provide independent expert analysis, including adverse framing. Missing but potentially useful later sources include DOD after-action or press briefing text, a direct video/transcript of President Trump's transition-control statement, and fuller mapping of exact ground-control sites.
technical_reports.title: Plaintiff Source and Weight Ledger

technical_reports.summary: Defendant inspected the record with get_case/list_evidence and read all five plaintiff text exhibits with read_evidence_range. Defendant submitted and read back two official defense exhibits: Department of War/Pentagon News on Operation Absolute Resolve and the Lead IG Operation Southern Spear report extract. Additional searches found DOJ and War/State leads but they were not submitted because the admitted official record already captured the key law-enforcement/counter-narcotics framing. Weight assessment: PX-2, DX-1, and DX-2 are official and strong for source identity; PX-5 is credible expert commentary but not a primary operational order or full transcript. Unresolved gaps include the full Trump transition-control video/transcript, full State transcript, and post-operation facts about who actually governed territory.
technical_reports.title: Defense Source and Weight Ledger

technical_reports.summary: Defendant inspected get_case and list_evidence before filing. Plaintiff rebuttal added no new evidence; it relied on existing DX-1, DX-2, and PX-5. Prior exact reads of those text exhibits remain controlling. No new external source was submitted because the rebuttal turned on interpretation of the existing record, not a new factual source.
technical_reports.title: Surrebuttal Evidence Check
